adventure.identity: A primarily exploration-driven game where progression is gated by acquired tools, knowledge, or environmental traversal rather than by leveling or stat-grinding (Zelda BOTW, Hollow Knight, Outer Wilds).
progression_systems: tool_unlocks (each tool opens new geography — Zelda key items)
ability_upgrades (double-jump, dash, glide — Hollow Knight charms / BOTW runes)
knowledge_progression (Outer Wilds: rumours / ship-log entries gate the player, not items)
world_state_flags (gates open, NPCs move, time-of-day)
health_capacity_increases (heart containers / mask shards)
equipment_durability_or_swap (BOTW weapons break, Hollow Knight charms slot)
currency (rupees / geo) for incremental upgrades — never the primary gate
completion_percent (collectibles, shrines, lore tablets)
win_conditions: Reach and defeat the final-boss / final-encounter
Reach a story end-state (Outer Wilds: solve the mystery and trigger the eye sequence)
Optional: 100% completion epilogue (Hollow Knight: True Ending requires Pantheon + Dream No More)
lose_conditions: Health depleted -> respawn at last bench/shrine/save point
Falling into hazard / out-of-bounds (lava, pit, void)
Story-specific timer expires (Outer Wilds: 22-min supernova loop — soft loss, resets the loop, knowledge persists)
npc_types: innkeeper / save-point host (Hollow Knight: bench-keeper variants)
enemy_types: small_grunt (rats, husks, chuchus) — slow approach + light contact damage
ranged_pest (archer, spitter) — fires projectiles, low HP
shielded_bruiser — must be flanked or stunned first
flying_harasser (keese, aspids) — dive attack, hard to hit on ground
ambusher (stalfos, peeping mantis) — pops out of environment
elite_mini-boss — telegraph + phase change
named_boss — multi-phase, arena-specific mechanics
hud: temperature / oxygen / weather indicator (BOTW, Outer Wilds)
iconic_mechanics: Tool-gated traversal: a new item unlocks new map regions (Zelda's hookshot/bombs; Hollow Knight's mantis claw / monarch wings)
Knowledge-as-progression: Outer Wilds locks nothing behind items — only behind what the player has read in the ship-log
Environmental storytelling: lore is embedded in geometry, murals, and ambient detail rather than in text dumps
common_bugs_anti_patterns: Out-of-bounds geometry — player falls forever, no respawn trigger (Game-Breaking Bug archetype)
Soft-locked door: the script that unlocks it never fires because the player triggered the cutscene from the wrong angle
Save corruption when saving inside a transient state (mid-cutscene, mid-dialog)
Dialog softlock: pressing skip during a typewriter effect cancels the next-line trigger and freezes input
Music layer never resolves (combat track keeps playing after enemies die because the 'leave-combat' check fires before all enemies are despawned)
Tool-gate logic checks for the wrong inventory flag and locks endgame content
Camera clipping into walls in tight corridors (3D)
Picking up a key item in the wrong order skips a story flag set by the cutscene

phaser: Use Tilemap (Tiled JSON) for rooms; one Phaser.Scene per persistent area; door/transition objects swap scenes preserving player inventory in a Registry singleton.
Dialog box: rexUI Dialog plugin or hand-rolled Container with typewriter Text + advance-on-Enter. State machine: IDLE -> TYPING -> AWAITING_INPUT -> ADVANCE.
Combat: same-scene; spawn enemy sprites with arcade physics; player attack hitbox is a short-lived sensor.
Save: serialize Registry (inventory, flags, lastSavePointId) to localStorage as JSON. Never serialize Phaser game objects directly.
three_js: Third-person camera: lerp camera position toward (player.position + offset), lookAt(player.position + headOffset). Use OrbitControls only as scaffolding — replace with custom follow camera before shipping.
Character controller: capsule collider (Rapier or Cannon-es), gravity, grounded check via short downward ray, jump impulse on grounded + jumpPressed.
Use AnimationMixer for skinned-mesh animations; cross-fade idle <-> run <-> jump.
Streaming: load region GLBs on demand, dispose old region's geometries/materials/textures to avoid GPU leaks.

rpg.identity: A party-based, story-driven game where progression is primarily numerical (XP, levels, equipment) and combat is resolved through menu-driven turn-based or ATB (Active Time Battle) commands (Final Fantasy, Chrono Trigger, Mother 3).

controls.mouse: Optional in classic JRPG — used in modern remakes for menu navigation only; not required.

player_abilities: save (at save points or anywhere depending on tradition)
progression_systems: experience_points (XP) — earned from each battle
character_level — caps stats and learnable abilities
stats: HP, MP/SP, ATK, DEF, MAG_ATK, MAG_DEF, SPD, LUCK
equipment: weapon, armor, helmet, accessory (party-member-specific slots)
spell/skill_unlocks (level-based or job-based)
job_or_class system (FF3/5/Tactics tradition)
story_flags (which chapter, which NPCs available, which areas open)
party_roster (acquire and lose members across the story)
gold / gil
limit_break / overdrive meter (modern FF)
affinity / relationship values (Mother 3, modern JRPGs)
win_conditions: Defeat final boss and watch ending cutscene
Optional: defeat super-boss / post-game content (Ozma, Lavos extra phases)
lose_conditions: Party wipe (all combat-active members at 0 HP) — Game Over screen
Story-mandated loss is usually NOT a Game Over (the story continues — e.g. Final Fantasy I forced losses)
Specific instant-kill effects on a required member during scripted fight
scenes: MainMenu (party / status / equip / magic / items / config / save)
npc_types: shopkeeper (weapon, armor, item, magic — typically split)
enemy_types: slime / flan — iconic weakest tier; iconic action: split when hit by physical, weak to ice/fire (Dragon Quest / FF tradition)
skeleton / undead — iconic action: revive after death (counter with holy/cure)
goblin / imp — basic melee, may steal gold
wolf / dire-wolf — fast, attacks twice per turn
bat — flying, hard to hit physically (counter: ranged/magic)
kobold mage — casts low-tier elemental spells
knight / armored — high DEF, weak to magic
ghost / wraith — physical-immune; magic-only
treasure_chest_mimic — disguised; iconic gotcha
tonberry — slow approach, one-shot when adjacent (iconic FF anti-archetype)
cactuar — high evasion, runs away, drops rare loot
chocobo / monster-mount — encountered before tameable
elemental (fire/ice/thunder/water) — weak to opposite element
dragon — high HP, breath attack hits party-wide (iconic boss-tier)
behemoth — hits whole party; iconic counter-attack on final blow (Final Strike)
named_boss (4 fiends / 4 archfiends / final boss) — multi-phase
inventory_categories: accessory (1-2 slots — status immunity, stat boost)

battle: status_effect_icons (poison, sleep, paralyze, confuse, silence, blind, petrify, doom)

iconic_mechanics: ATB / turn-based menu combat: command queue resolves on a per-character readiness gauge (Final Fantasy IV-IX) or strict turn order (Dragon Quest, Chrono Trigger)
Random encounters OR on-map encounters that trigger battle scene transition (Chrono Trigger eliminated random encounters and let battles happen on the map without transition — a defining design)
Limit / dual / triple techniques: combo abilities that require multiple party members to be ready and aligned (Chrono Trigger's signature — explicitly emphasized team synergy)
Status condition layer: poison/sleep/paralyze/confuse/silence/blind/petrify/doom — each with explicit per-turn behaviour and counter-item

music_slots: victory_fanfare (must NOT loop on win — short sting then resume field music)

common_bugs_anti_patterns: Battle music doesn't stop after victory: the 'leave-combat' transition didn't fire; happens when a new party member is added without resetting audio context, or when the player flees mid-track
Dialog softlock: skip-key skips the line that arms the next trigger; player input freezes
Item duplication: dropping an equipped item mid-equip-screen leaves a stale reference in the inventory array
Save corruption: saving during a battle scene transition writes a half-loaded state
XP overflow: at very high levels integer overflows into negative — instant level-up loop or freeze
Status effect persists after KO + revive (poison ticks the resurrected character to 0 immediately)
Permanently missable content: a chest in a one-way dungeon that disappears after the boss — pipeline-level missable check
Encounter rate stays elevated after the player equips an Encounter-Down accessory (flag set but not read)
Critical-hit floor: damage calculated before defense subtraction can underflow on weak enemies and heal them
Wrong character speaks a line because the dialog system uses the leader-slot index and the player swapped party order

implementation_notes.engine: Phaser 3 — proven track for classic 2D JRPG (GameDev Academy + html5gamedevelopment + zenva tutorial series).
phaser_pattern: FieldScene: Tiled tilemap for the world; player sprite with Arcade physics; invisible encounter zones trigger BattleScene.start().

BattleScene + UIScene running concurrently: BattleScene owns Unit instances (extend Phaser.GameObjects.Sprite, expose hp/mp/atk/def/spd); UIScene owns the menus and overlay HUD. Use scene.events to communicate (UIScene listens 'commandSelected'; BattleScene listens 'turnReady').

Turn engine: priority queue ordered by (readiness_gauge_full, then SPD, then random tiebreak). On each tick in update(), advance every unit's gauge by dt * unit.spd; when a player unit reaches full, pause and request input via UIScene.

Scene stacking: when BattleScene starts, FieldScene.scene.pause() preserves the field state; on victory, BattleScene.scene.stop() and FieldScene.scene.resume() — never re-create FieldScene or you lose flags.
Persist: Registry (game.registry) for inventory, party, gold, story flags. Save = JSON.stringify(registry.values) -> localStorage. Reload = registry.merge(parsed).
implementation_notes.dialog_system: Use rexUI Dialog plugin OR a custom Container with typewriter Text and a fixed pump pattern: state machine TYPING -> WAIT_INPUT -> ADVANCE -> NEXT_PAGE -> CLOSE. Block field input while open by setting a global registry flag 'dialogOpen'.

3d-arpg.identity: An isometric / fixed-camera 3D action-RPG built around real-time click-to-move-and-attack combat, randomized loot, and skill-tree character progression — the Diablo / Torchlight lineage, distinct from over-the-shoulder 3D ARPGs (Souls/Witcher) and from 2D ARPGs.

keyboard.1-4: skill slots (most builds use 4-6 active skills)

gamepad.left_stick: move (modern entries support direct stick movement; classic Diablo was mouse-only)

player_abilities: basic_attack (primary skill — replaces autoattack from level 1)
progression_systems: experience_points and character_level (typically 1-60 to 1-100)
skill_points or skill_unlocks (per level — fuel the skill tree / runeword / passive node graph)
paragon_levels (post-cap infinite XP — Diablo III/IV signature)
attribute_points (Strength / Dexterity / Intelligence / Vitality — manual in D2/Torchlight, automatic in D3/D4)

gear_loot: items roll random affixes; rarity tiers White / Blue (Magic) / Yellow (Rare) / Orange (Legendary) / Gold (Unique). Magic = 1 affix; Rare = 2-6 affixes; Legendary = 4 affixes plus a powerful Legendary affix; Unique = fixed unique effect, only one Unique equippable at a time (Diablo IV).
set_items (gold/green) — bonus when multiple set pieces equipped
magic_find / item_quantity stat (boosts loot drop rarity)
gem_sockets and runes
crafting_materials (salvaged from gear)
endgame: Sacred / Ancestral tiers, seasonal leagues, scalable dungeons (Maps / Greater Rifts / Pinnacle bosses)
currency: gold + crafting materials + endgame currencies (D2 runes, PoE orbs)
win_conditions: Complete the campaign (defeat final act boss)
But: campaign is the on-ramp — endgame begins at max level. Progression is open-ended (push higher rifts / maps / leagues / seasons)
Optional: complete season journey, achieve seasonal title, fill set / unique collection
lose_conditions: HP -> 0 — death respawns at last checkpoint / town with optional XP penalty
Hardcore mode: HP -> 0 = permanent character deletion (genre staple)
Disconnect during boss fight (online entries) — variable handling
enemy_types: trash_melee (skeleton, fallen, zombie) — swarm, low HP, contact damage
trash_ranged (archer skeleton, dark cultist) — kites, fragile
trash_caster (shaman, sorcerer) — fireball / curse, must be priority-killed
fast_runner (ghoul, fallen-imp) — interrupts kiting
exploder (suicide bomber, corpse) — runs in, detonates
summoner (necromancer) — raises waves of trash
brute (large melee, slow but high damage)
shielded_or_armored (must be back-attacked or stagger-broken)
champion_pack (3-5 enemies sharing one elite_affix — Molten / Mortar / Fire-Chains / Electrified / Frozen / Vortex / Jailer / Arcane-Enchanted)
rare_elite (single enemy with 2-4 affixes; on Diablo III: 1 affix at lvl 1-29, 2 at 30-49, 3 at 50-59, 4 at 60+)
treasure_goblin (loot-pinata, runs to portal — Diablo III signature)
named_act_boss (multi-phase, arena hazards)
world_boss (open-zone scheduled spawn)
rift_guardian (endgame Greater Rift terminator)
inventory_categories: armor: helm, chest, gloves, boots, belt, pants
hud: mana_or_resource_globe (right) — class-specific resource (Wrath, Fury, Hatred, Mana, Energy)
skill_bar (4-6 active slots with cooldown rings)
iconic_mechanics: Loot loop: kill -> randomized drops -> compare affixes -> equip / salvage -> repeat. Magic Find / Item Quantity stats explicitly tilt the random tables.

Affix-driven elite packs: Champion / Rare / Unique monsters get bonus 'Elite Affixes' (Molten leaves a damaging trail, Mortar lobs ranged AoE, Fire-Chains links pack members, Electrified reflects sparks on hit, Frozen drops freeze pylons). Number of affixes scales with hero level.
Endgame-first design: campaign is on-ramp; the real game begins at max level via seasonal leagues, scalable dungeons, pinnacle bosses, and crafting progression.
Pet courier (Torchlight signature): companion runs back to town to sell trash loot so the player never leaves combat for inventory.
common_bugs_anti_patterns: Loot table mis-rolls: probability sum > 1.0 due to floating-point, drops are heavily skewed
Item duplication via inventory drag race condition (online) — must lock both source and target slots before transfer
Affix stacking exploit: Legendary affix multiplies an already-multiplicative stat -> infinite damage
Stuck-in-geometry: pathfinding gets player or pet wedged into a wall corner
Elite-pack affix combo creates unwinnable scenarios for melee (Frozen + Molten + Jailer all firing in a corridor)
Memory leak from never disposing dropped-item visual after auto-pickup
Skill cooldown not reset on death -> respawn with skills permanently on cooldown
Town-portal drops player into combat zone unexpectedly because waypoint position was overwritten
Hardcore false-death from server lag (client says alive, server says dead) -> permanent character loss complaint
Damage-over-time keeps ticking after enemy is dead, popping a 0-damage number forever

implementation_notes.engine: Three.js with isometric (orthographic) camera and physics via Rapier or Cannon-es. For pure 2D-projected isometric, Phaser 3 is also viable but you lose true 3D.
three_js_pattern: Camera: OrthographicCamera at fixed angle (~30 deg pitch, fixed yaw). Camera follows player by lerping camera.position toward (player.position + cameraOffset) every frame; never parent the camera to the player (rotation snapping looks bad).
Click-to-move: raycaster against ground plane on left-click; A* (or NavMesh-based pathfinding via three-pathfinding lib) computes path; player walks along path, abandons it on new click.
Combat: target acquisition via raycast on enemies; primary skill slot is 'auto-attack-on-target'; skills 2-6 fire-on-keypress with cooldown UI.

Loot generation: server (or deterministic client) rolls a loot table per-kill: roll rarity tier (weighted with magic-find), then per-affix-roll within the rarity's affix pool. Drop a Mesh + label that floats above ground until clicked.
Skill bar: HUD as DOM overlay or as a separate Three.js scene with OrthographicCamera; cooldown ring is a circular mask animated via shader or via SVG arc.
Save: serialize character (level, skills, inventory item[]) to localStorage or to a backend. Items are { baseId, rarity, affixes:[{stat,value}] }, never serialize the Mesh.
implementation_notes.phaser_alternative: If the target scope is small (browser game), Phaser 3 with sprite isometric tiles (e.g. phaser3-isometric plugin) is faster to ship but limits camera flexibility. The Diablo / Torchlight feel of true 3D depth comes through more cleanly in Three.js.

3d-platformer.identity: A 3D action-platformer built around character traversal through hub-and-level worlds, with collectible-driven progression and a player-controlled or designer-anchored 3rd-person camera (Mario 64, Banjo-Kazooie, Astro Bot).

keyboard.F: use ability / context-action (Banjo: peck/beak-buster, Mario: punch)

controls.mouse: Right stick equivalent — controls the third-person camera (yaw/pitch). Left mouse can serve as primary action.

gamepad.A_or_cross: jump (tap = jump, double-tap = double-jump, triple-tap-while-running = triple-jump in Mario tradition)

player_abilities: triple_jump (Mario 64 signature: rhythmic A-press while running yields highest leap with a vocalized cue on the third)
long_jump (Run + Crouch + Jump: Z + A in Mario 64)
wall_kick / wall_jump (jump into wall, press jump exactly on contact)
ground_pound (in air -> crouch / B; cancels fall damage if timed near ground)
glide (Banjo's flight pad / Mario's wing cap / Astro's hover)
progression_systems: primary_collectible_count: Power Stars (Mario 64) / Jiggies (Banjo) — gates access to new hub doors and the final boss
secondary_collectible: Coins (Mario) / Musical Notes (Banjo: 810/900 minimum to clear all doors) / Bots (Astro)
ability_unlocks: gradually expand the verb list (Banjo learns moves from Bottles; Mario gets caps; Astro gets new gadgets)
health_capacity: 8 HP -> larger via collectibles (Banjo's honeycomb pieces -> 1 extra unit per 6 honeycombs)
lives (classic — Mario 64 had 1-up mushrooms; modern entries often drop the lives mechanic)
world_unlocks: collectible thresholds open new hub doors / portals
boss_keys / story_flags
completion_percent (e.g., 100% requires 120/120 stars in Mario 64; 100/100 jiggies in Banjo)
win_conditions: Reach the required collectible threshold and defeat the final boss (Mario 64: 70/120 Power Stars to enter Bowser's final stage; Banjo-Kazooie: 94/100 Jiggies and 810/900 Notes to reach Grunty)
100% completion is the optional / true ending
lose_conditions: HP -> 0 -> Game Over OR exit-to-hub (modern). Mario 64 kicks Mario out of the level on death.
Falling out-of-bounds / into bottomless pit -> instant kill
Time limit on specific challenge (Mario 64 timed-star runs)
scenes: Hub_World (Peach's Castle / Spiral Mountain / Tar Tarus)
Level (per-world, often non-linear sandbox or linear obstacle course)
SubLevel (mini-stage inside a level — painting world)
npc_types: quest_giver (level inhabitants asking for help -> Star/Jiggy reward)
rescue_target (5 captured Jinjos per level in Banjo)
racer / time-trial NPC (Mario 64: Koopa the Quick)
warp_npc (Lakitu cameraman — meta NPC for camera control)
enemy_types: goomba / mumbo-style grunt — patrol, contact damage, stomp-defeated
koopa / shelled — stomp removes shell; can be picked up and thrown
spiked (urchin, spike-helmet) — cannot stomp; must hit with secondary ability
flying (paratroopa, bee, lakitu) — drop projectiles; defeated by jumping into them
ground_pound_only (chain chomp, big bully) — must use ground-pound or push into hazard
fire / lava (bowser-flame, podoboo)
water_enemy (eel, urchin in coral)
bouncing_explosive (bob-omb)
stationary_hazard (thwomp, fire-bar)
mini_boss (King Bob-omb / Big Bully / Eyerock) — multi-hit, telegraphed attack
world_boss (Bowser / Grunty) — multi-phase arena fight
hud: minimap (rare in genre — most rely on navigable hub)
objective_pop_up (one-line star/jiggy hint at level entry)
iconic_mechanics: Move-set as toolkit: every level is solvable with the same ~12 verbs (jump / triple-jump / long-jump / wall-kick / ground-pound / dive / punch / swim) — level design is built around composing these verbs

Hub-and-spoke level structure: a central hub gates levels behind collectible counts; each level is a small sandbox with multiple stars/jiggies (Mario 64 popularized; Banjo refined by keeping the player IN the level across stars rather than ejecting after each)

Player-controlled 3rd-person camera with snap-points for tricky platforms (Lakitu-cam in Mario 64; explicit C-button camera in Banjo). Modern entries auto-frame the camera while still allowing manual override. Fiddly camera in jumping sections is a documented genre weakness — design around it.

sfx_required: note_collect (ascending pitches per consecutive — Banjo signature)
primary_collectible_get_fanfare (Star/Jiggy jingle — must be musical, not a generic SFX)
music_slots: underwater (often a remix of the level theme — Mario 64 staple)
powerup_short_loop (caps duration of a star-cap / wing-cap)

common_bugs_anti_patterns: Camera clipping into walls in narrow corridors — must use a spring-arm raycast that retracts the camera when occluded
Camera overshoot on rapid 180-turn — lerp factor too high; tune camera follow speed below player turn speed
Out-of-bounds: player squeezes through a vertex seam, falls forever — backstop with a kill-Y plane

Double-jump triggered after walking off a ledge -> air-jump-from-coyote-window stacks with double-jump and produces infinite jump; check 'wasGroundedRecently' OR 'doubleJumpUsed' but not both incorrectly
Ground-pound + wall-kick desync: ground-pound flag never clears so subsequent jumps register as 'still pounding'
Picking up an object cancels mid-air collision so the player drops through a moving platform
Lakitu-style auto-camera frames the player into a tree right before a tricky jump
Collectible counter persists across files because it's stored on the global Registry not the per-file slot
Triple-jump only fires when running on perfectly flat ground because slope-test floating-point gives < 1.0 grounded normal
Audio track for a transient cap (wing-cap) keeps looping after the powerup expires

implementation_notes.engine: Three.js + a physics layer (Rapier preferred for capsule/character-controller; Cannon-es viable for simpler scope).
character_controller_pattern: Capsule collider (player). Move via velocity = (inputDir * speed) + (0, verticalVel, 0). Apply gravity each frame.
Grounded-check: short downward raycast from capsule base. isGrounded sets coyote-time window (~0.1s) for late jump-press grace.

Jump state machine: GROUNDED -> JUMP_1 (impulse) -> AIR -> JUMP_2 (only if doubleJumpUnlocked && !doubleJumpUsed) -> LAND. Triple-jump requires running speed > threshold AND consecutive-jump counter resetting on land within a small window.
Ground-pound: while in AIR, on Crouch press -> set verticalVel = -large; lock horizontal input; on contact -> spawn shockwave + damage AOE around player.
Wall-kick: while in AIR and side-collision detected, on Jump press within ~0.15s of contact -> set verticalVel positive AND horizontal component away from wall.
camera_pattern: Third-person follow camera: target = player.position + headOffset; desiredCamPos = target + Quaternion(yaw,pitch) * (0,0, -distance). Each frame, lerp camera.position toward desiredCamPos with a speed slightly slower than the player turn speed for that 'follow string' feeling described in Mario 64's camera.
Spring arm: raycast from target toward desiredCamPos; if hit < distance, snap camera to hit point - small offset to avoid clipping into walls.
Manual override: right-stick (or mouse) adjusts yaw/pitch deltas; auto-recenter behind player after N seconds of no input.
C-button snap-points (Banjo style): designer-placed CameraVolume zones force a specific yaw/pitch for tricky platforming sections.
implementation_notes.level_authoring: Each level is a separate Three.js scene OR a streaming chunk; the Hub is its own scene that warps to a level scene via portal collider. Persist player state (collectibles, abilities, lives) in a global Registry / Zustand store, never in the scene itself.

phaser_3: 2D adventure (Hollow-Knight-like): Tilemap rooms, scene-per-region, persistent player Registry.
JRPG: dual-scene battle (BattleScene + UIScene), Unit class extending Sprite, ATB/turn priority queue, Registry-backed save/load.
Dialog: rexUI Dialog plugin or hand-rolled Container with typewriter Text and TYPING/WAIT_INPUT/ADVANCE state machine.
three_js: 3D adventure / 3D platformer: capsule character controller via Rapier, grounded-check raycast, third-person spring-arm camera, region streaming with dispose-on-unload to avoid GPU memory leaks.

Isometric ARPG: OrthographicCamera at fixed angle, raycaster click-to-move with NavMesh pathfinding, randomized loot tables driven by rarity-weighted roll then per-affix roll, HUD as DOM overlay or separate orthographic scene.
